TextENGNew: The light falls on Arakawa. Mion, Rika, Tonegawa, Hoshino, and Okano enter, their faces blank. They slowly encircle him, keeping their distance.
"......Wh... What do you want...?!"
"You attended Yanagawa Municipal Elementary School, didn't you. ...It was when you were in the third grade, wasn't it?"
"...You had the misfortune of being caught short on the way home from school, and so you crept into the bushes to relieve yourself."
"Hey! Arakawa's doing it outside! He's doing it in the bushes!"
changeReason: Old says 'shitting outside' (explicit); new says 'doing it outside / in the bushes' (euphemistic). While the crude humor is part of the scene, the new version softens the language significantly, matching the less explicit TextJPN 野グソ.

TextENGNew: "What the—! What's your problem?! ...You'd do the same if there was no bathroom around!"
"No I wouldn't! Normal people don't do that."
changeReason: Old says 'I generally wouldn't' (qualifier of frequency); new says 'Normal people don't do that' (normative judgment). TextJPN 普通はしない means 'normally one wouldn't,' supporting the new version's normative framing.

TextENGNew: "I would absolutely find a proper bathroom."
"Doing it outside... How disgusting!"
changeReason: Old says 'gross' (casual); new says 'How disgusting' (more formal/emphatic). TextJPN 下劣 means 'vulgar/base,' which is closer to 'disgusting' than 'gross.'

TextENGNew: "Oh! He's coming this way, he's coming this way! Shitman's coming!"
Everyone but Yoshimura and Arakawa bursts out laughing.
"Shitman was quite the hit, wasn't it? ...It spread like wildfire. Fourth grade, fifth grade, sixth grade — no matter what class you moved to, that name followed you."
"The girl who coined that name was Hirayama Yuuko-san. ...And how you hated her for it."
"...If she hadn't given you that name, none of this would have happened to you."
"...So you attacked her. Over and over again with a broomstick handle. Even when she cried, you kept hitting her."
"Arakawa-kun! Hitting a girl — how despicable!"
"Waaaaaaaaahhn! Arakawa hit me!"
"You're the worst!"
changeReason: Old translates 最低男 as 'Scumbag'; new translates it as 'You're the worst.' 'Scumbag' has a stronger, more specific derogatory connotation than the literal meaning 'worst man.'

TextENGNew: "You shouldn't get worked up over a girl!"
changeReason: Old says 'There's no reason to do that to a girl'; new says 'You shouldn't get worked up over a girl.' TextJPN 女なんかにマジになんなよ means 'don't get serious over a girl,' supporting the new translation's meaning.

TextENGNew: "Hey, Arakawa. You can let your teacher know quietly. ...You like Hirayama, don't you? Don't you?"
changeReason: Old has the teacher say 'tell me, your teacher, in secret'; new says 'let your teacher know quietly.' More importantly, the nuance of the teasing question differs -- both are mocking but the old is slightly more role-play while new is more casual.

TextENGNew: "Uooooooooooooooooooh!"
"In middle school, Shitman continued to thrive. ...Having strangers look at you with that curious, knowing stare must have been agony."
"That him? ...The one they call Shitman?"
"Better keep your distance! ...I heard he smells!"
"Ahahahahaha! Filthy, filthy, filthy!"
"Shut up! That only happened once! Shut up about it!"
"The long ordeal of compulsory schooling finally ended, and at last you were able to make a fresh start in high school."
"Not a single person from your old middle school was in your year. You breathed a sigh of relief."
".........Hey, you! ...Are you... the Shitman?"
"What?! ......What?!"
"Even now, no one knows how word got out. But... someone, somewhere, had heard the name."
changeReason: Old says 'how that name got passed from school to school'; new says 'no one knows how word got out.' Old specifies school-to-school transmission; new is more general, matching TextJPN which just says どこから伝わったのか (where it came from).

TextENGNew: Everyone but Yoshimura and Arakawa continues laughing. Arakawa grips his umbrella and hauls himself unsteadily to his feet.
"Shut up shut up SHUT UP! ...Be quiet! I'll kill you if you don't shut up!"
"If you hit a girl, you'd instantly be cast as the villain. So you ground your teeth and endured it."
"Idiot! I'm not holding back just because you're a woman!"
Arakawa advances on Mion and Rika, swinging his umbrella wildly. The two back away, doubled over with laughter.
"If you hit a boy, he'd retaliate immediately. So again you ground your teeth and endured it."
"Don't mess with me! I'll kill you! I'll kill every last one of you!"
changeReason: Old says 'I don't care who you are, I'll kill you'; new says 'I'll kill every last one of you.' TextJPN 誰だろうと殺してやる means 'I'll kill you no matter who you are' / 'I'll kill anyone,' and the new captures the 'everyone' sense better.

TextENGNew: Arakawa turns on Tonegawa, Hoshino, and Okano, still swinging his umbrella. All three back away, laughing.
Arakawa stands at center stage, swinging his umbrella in helpless rage. The other five laugh harder than ever.
"I'll kill you! I'll kill all of you! Every last one of you! You filthy bastards! ...Waaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaah!"
"It's anguish, isn't it. It's torment! ...Don't you want to reach out for it? ...For salvation!"
changeReason: Old uses past tense 'It must have been painful'; new uses present tense 'It's anguish, isn't it.' TextJPN uses present tense (苦しいでしょう), and the temporal framing changes the scene's immediacy.

TextENGNew: "...You must want it! ...You do, don't you!"
"Everyone who laughs at me dies! ...Uooooooooooooooooh!"
"...Aren't you starting to want... to meet it? ......Fufufufu!"
changeReason: Old says 'Do you want to meet them now?'; new says 'Aren't you starting to want to meet it?' 'Them' (plural, people) vs 'it' (singular, entity/Oyashiro-sama) changes the referent. TextJPN uses no pronoun but context implies Oyashiro-sama.

TextENGNew: "Meet what?!"
changeReason: Old says 'Meet who?!'; new says 'Meet what?!' -- 'who' implies a person, 'what' implies a thing or entity, consistent with the 'it' vs 'them' distinction.

TextENGNew: "...Kondou-san and Kakinuma-san both suffered a great deal, you know. But they were freed. ...Just like Mori-san and Katou-san!"
"Heh! ...Hehehe! ......Is that so...! ...So that's what this is all about...!"
"...You want to meet it, don't you? ...You want to meet it!"
changeReason: Old says 'You want to meet them, right?'; new says 'You want to meet it, don't you?' -- same 'them' vs 'it' referent change as 156279.

TextENGNew: "Fine then, come at me. ...I'll kill everyone who laughs at me! ...The curse? What kind of joke is that! What do I care about Oyashiro-sama!"
changeReason: Old says 'Screw the curse! So what about this Oyashiro-sama crap?!'; new says 'The curse? What kind of joke is that! What do I care about Oyashiro-sama!' -- different framing of defiance.

TextENGNew: "Ahahahaha! Fine! I'll meet it! ...And then I'll kill every single one of them!"
changeReason: Old says 'I'll go ahead and meet them, then! And I'll kill every last one of them!'; new says 'I'll meet it! And then I'll kill every single one of them!' -- 'them' (the entity) vs 'it' for Oyashiro-sama; who he'll kill differs.

TextENGNew: "You want to meet it? ......You want to meet it. ......You want to meet it?!"
changeReason: Consistent 'them' vs 'it' change for the Oyashiro-sama referent across all three repetitions.

TextENGNew: "......Hahahaha! Ahhhahahahahahaha!"
"...You said it, didn't you? ...You want to meet it? ...Hahahahahaha!"
"Then I'll arrange a meeting for you. ......With Oyashiro-sama......!"
changeReason: Old says 'I'll help you meet them... Oyashiro-sama'; new says 'I'll arrange a meeting... With Oyashiro-sama' -- 'them' vs singular entity, and 'help you meet' vs 'arrange a meeting' differs in agency.
